key_departures.impact: Super Bowl LX MVP. Departed as the highest-paid RB in league history at time of signing. Zach Charbonnet (recovering from late-season ACL) is the presumed RB1, but is not ready for Week 1. Only low-cost RBs remain on the depth chart (Holani, McIntosh, Akers). Massive production hole to fill.

4-year starter and key piece of Seattle's top-ranked scoring defense. Posted 7 interceptions and 13 pass breakups across 2025 season and playoffs. Departure creates a real void at safety alongside Julian Love. Ty Okada (tendered) is the likely replacement but is unproven as a starter.

Generated 146 pressures over 3 seasons as one of Seattle's primary pass rushers. Leaving creates a legitimate need at EDGE alongside Uchenna Nwosu and DeMarcus Lawrence. The 2026 draft class is reportedly deep at EDGE, but this is a real production gap. DeMarcus Lawrence (signed 2025) remains; Mike Morris and Nwosu are the next options.

Former Pro Bowler who had been overtaken on the depth chart by Josh Jobe in 2025. Departed on a one-year prove-it deal, widely expected by the team. CB2 spot opposite Jobe (re-signed) remains open. Devon Witherspoon is the CB1. Loss escalates need for CB depth/CB2.
Special teams contributor and depth WR. Signed with new Raiders HC Klint Kubiak (former SEA OC). WR room remains deep with JSN, Kupp, Shaheed, Horton, and tendered Bobo. Departure is low-impact.
Special teams LB. Drake Thomas (re-signed) and Ernest Jones IV remain as the starting duo. Surratt was a ST contributor only. Departure is minimal impact; LB depth needs monitoring.

signings.previous_team: Seattle Seahawks (re-sign; acquired via trade from Saints in Nov 2025)
signings.analysis: Re-signing a key piece of the Super Bowl-winning team. Shaheed was the only player in the NFL with kick return and punt return TDs in the 2025 regular season. His $17M AAV is mid-tier for WR but elite for a returner/flex receiver. $34.7M guaranteed signals a real commitment. WR was not a pre-FA need — Shaheed's return solidifies a deep room (JSN, Kupp, Shaheed, Horton). No need impact but prevents one from forming.

Jobe was arguably the best value signing of Seattle's early FA period. He posted the 4th-lowest completion percentage allowed in the NFL in 2025 (47.7%) and started 15 of 16 games. At $8M AAV, this is below starter CB market (~$12-20M AAV) but reflects Jobe's first full season as a starter. The 3-year deal with $7M guaranteed (signing bonus) is a real commitment but only the signing bonus is fully locked in — back-year salaries are not guaranteed. Locks in the CB1 opposite Devon Witherspoon. However, Woolen's departure still leaves a CB2/nickel void, and the limited guaranteed structure suggests the team views this more as a 2-year real commitment. CB need reduced but not eliminated — a CB2 or nickel corner is still needed in the draft or late free agency.

Thomas had a breakout 2025 season starting 14 of 17 games, posting 47 solo tackles, 8 PDs, 6 QB hits, 3.5 sacks. The 2-year, $8M deal is a bargain — well below the $8-16M starter tier for LBs. $3M guaranteed is modest but enough to show commitment through Year 1. He pairs with Ernest Jones IV to give Seattle a complete LB unit at minimal cost. LB was not a need and remains stable. Extension done in lieu of RFA tender to give Thomas guaranteed money.

First outside RB addition after losing Super Bowl MVP Kenneth Walker III. Wilson is a former Packers backup who rushed for 998 yards and 7 TDs over two seasons and filled in capably as a starter for injured Josh Jacobs in 2025. The 1-year, up to $2.1M deal is a minimum-tier signing. He's a capable RB2/3 but this does not come close to replacing Walker's explosiveness or volume. With Charbonnet (ACL) uncertain for Week 1, RB is still a significant need — this is a bridge/competition signing, not a solution. RB need remains tier 1-2.

Seattle's first external FA signing of the 2026 period, confirmed by GM John Schneider. Thomas is a 1-year deal at low cost (contract terms undisclosed but comp pick formula suggests sub-market). He started 25 games over his first two Colts seasons, with 6 career INTs and 114 tackles, but was relegated to a backup/special teams role over the past two years. He provides competition for a starting spot alongside Julian Love, with Ty Okada (tendered ERFA) also in the mix. One-year deal signals bridge/competition, not a long-term fix. Safety remains a genuine need for the draft after losing Coby Bryant.

signings.previous_team: Carolina Panthers (was SEA, waived, claimed by CAR late 2025)
signings.analysis: Bell is a familiar face who played 14 games for Seattle in 2025 with two starts before being waived and claimed by Carolina. Returns as a depth safety and special teams contributor. One-year deal at undisclosed terms. Adds secondary depth but is not a starter solution. Combined with Rodney Thomas II, the safety room now has Julian Love, Ty Okada, Bell, Thomas, and AJ Finley — but none of these are clear Coby Bryant replacements. S need persists.

Finley missed the entire 2025 season with a knee injury suffered in August. Returns on an undisclosed deal (minimum range) to provide depth and compete on special teams. His 2026 contribution is uncertain given the lengthy layoff. Low-cost move that has no meaningful impact on the safety need picture.

Depth nose tackle re-signed at a bargain rate. Was not tendered as an RFA (which would have cost $3.5M+), instead brought back at $2M. Pili averaged only 11.5 snaps per game in 2025 on one of the best defensive lines in football. Leonard Williams and Jarran Reed (both 30+) lead the group; Pili adds needed youth to the rotation. IDL was not a meaningful pre-FA need and remains unchanged. Low-cost, sensible retention.

2-year deal for the team's unofficial special teams captain and versatile TE/FB hybrid. Led all NFL players with 394 special teams snaps in the regular season and led the Seahawks with 14 ST tackles. Valued primarily for his leadership and special teams impact rather than offensive production. TE was not a pre-FA need (Noah Fant, AJ Barner, Arroyo remain); this is a retention of a key role player.

Top reserve OL re-signed on a 1-year, $4M deal with $3M guaranteed. Jones provides backup versatility at tackle and guard. The offensive line returns intact with Cross, Lucas, Zabel, Bradford, Sundell — Jones is the swing backup. IOL was not a pre-FA need. $3M of $4M guaranteed signals he's a real roster lock, not a training camp arm.

Right-of-first-refusal tender keeps the backup WR and fan favorite in the fold at $3.52M (unguaranteed). Bobo projects no higher than WR5 behind JSN, Kupp, Shaheed, and Horton. He's a tough run blocker and ST contributor. Team retains the right to match any offer sheet. WR depth maintained; if cut before camp it frees cap room.

signings.previous_team: Seattle Seahawks (extension signed Dec 2025 while on roster; signed originally March 2025)
signings.analysis: Already under contract via a mid-season 1-year extension signed December 2025 when activated from IR (calf injury). He was the team's depth TE and special teams contributor in 2025. Listed here for completeness — this was not a 2026 free agency move but an in-season extension that carries into 2026.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Loss of Super Bowl MVP Kenneth Walker III (to KC, 3yr/$43M) creates an immediate, critical need. Zach Charbonnet is recovering from a late-season ACL and may not be ready for Week 1. The only current healthy depth is George Holani (ERFA tender), Emanuel Wilson (1yr/$2.1M, depth signing), Kenny McIntosh, and Cam Akers. Wilson's signing is a bridge move, not a solution. RB is now a top-2 draft/FA priority.

Boye Mafe's departure to Cincinnati (3yr/$60M) removes one of Seattle's primary pass rushers (146 career pressures in 3 seasons). DeMarcus Lawrence (signed 2025), Uchenna Nwosu, and Mike Morris remain, but Lawrence is 34 and Nwosu has battled injuries. No external EDGE signed in 2026 FA. With the 2026 draft reportedly deep at EDGE, this is a strong Day 1-2 draft target. Not yet tier 1 because of the Lawrence/Nwosu depth, but EDGE is now a legitimate prioritized need.

Josh Jobe re-signed (3yr/$24M) locks in one starting outside CB. Devon Witherspoon (CB1, extension-eligible) is under contract. But Riq Woolen's departure (Eagles, 1yr/$15M prove-it) leaves the CB2/nickel role open. Jobe's deal — only $7M guaranteed (signing bonus only, back-year salaries not fully guaranteed) — means Seattle still views this position as needing long-term investment. CB need is unchanged in tier: still need a CB2/nickel corner from the draft or late FA. Tier stays at 2.

Coby Bryant's departure (Bears, 3yr/$40M) creates a real starting safety need. The response — Rodney Thomas II (1yr, terms TBD), D'Anthony Bell (1yr, terms TBD), and AJ Finley (re-sign, undisclosed, coming off IR) — is all depth/competition signings on one-year deals. Julian Love is the CB1 safety; Ty Okada (ERFA tender) is the heir apparent but unproven as a starter. No starter-level safety has been added. S remains a tier 2 draft need — the draft or a later FA addition should be pursued.

Drake Thomas re-signed (2yr/$8M) pairs with Ernest Jones IV (multi-year deal signed 2025) to give Seattle a complete, starting-caliber LB duo at a bargain price. Both starters are locked up, and the position group has depth. LB drops from a minor need to virtually no need heading into the draft.

Rashid Shaheed re-signed (3yr/$51M, $34.7M gtd) to return alongside JSN, Kupp, and Horton. WR is now a position of strength. Dareke Young's departure (to Raiders) is easily absorbed. The 4-deep receiving core plus tendered Jake Bobo is among the strongest in the NFC. WR drops to a luxury pick; depth WR/developmental slot is the most one could justify drafting here.

Josh Jones re-signed (1yr/$4M, $3M gtd) as the swing backup. The starting OL of Cross, Lucas, Zabel, Bradford, Sundell returns intact from the Super Bowl. IOL is not a pressing need; drafting depth is always useful but not a priority.

key_additions.headline: Pro Bowl returner and receiver stays in Seattle after three return TDs helped win the Super Bowl
Starting CB locked up on a bargain deal after allowing the 4th-lowest completion rate in the NFL in 2025
Breakout starting linebacker retained at below-market value to anchor the defense alongside Ernest Jones IV
First outside RB addition after Walker's departure; career backup who stepped up as Packers starter in 2025
First external FA signing addresses Coby Bryant's void at safety; former starter with 6 career INTs
Familiar face returns to bolster safety depth and special teams after making two starts in 2025
Versatile OL reserve keeps the Super Bowl offensive line depth intact for another year
Special teams captain re-signs; led all NFL players with 394 ST snaps in the 2025 regular season
26-year-old depth DT kept at below-RFA-tender rate to inject youth into an aging D-line rotation

key_losses.headline: Super Bowl LX MVP departs as the biggest free agent loss; leaves a critical RB void with Charbonnet on ACL rehab
Primary pass rusher who produced 146 pressures in 3 seasons departs, leaving a significant EDGE need
Four-year defensive starter who posted 7 INTs and 13 PBUs in 2025 departs to Chicago, creating safety hole
Former Pro Bowl CB departs on a prove-it deal, leaving CB2/nickel role open heading into the draft
Special teams WR follows ex-Seahawks OC Klint Kubiak to Las Vegas; depth loss absorbed by returning WR room

notes: Seahawks' stated offseason strategy per GM John Schneider: prioritize retaining their own free agents over splashy external additions.

Seattle entered 2026 FA with approximately $58M in cap space (per Field Gulls/OverTheCap). Current post-FA space per OTC: ~$42.97M — still significant flexibility remaining for draft rookies and any late FA additions.
Zach Charbonnet (ACL) is the presumed RB1 when healthy but timeline for Week 1 availability is uncertain — this makes the RB need even more acute.
Devon Witherspoon and Jaxon Smith-Njigba are both extension-eligible and expected to command top-of-market deals in coming months; this will significantly affect remaining cap space.
Seahawks have only 4 draft picks in 2026; Schneider has indicated 2027 draft class is viewed as stronger and the team is managing compensatory pick formula accordingly.
Contract for Rodney Thomas II and D'Anthony Bell had not been fully disclosed as of March 13, 2026; estimated at below comp-pick formula threshold.

Shemar Jean-Charles, Eric Saubert, Steven Sims, Drew Lock, River Cracraft, and Josh Ross were all signed during the 2025 offseason (March 2025) and are already under contract heading into 2026 — they are not 2026 free agency moves and are excluded from this tracker accordingly.

Josh Jones contract per OverTheCap: 1yr/$4M with $3M guaranteed. Jobe contract per multiple reports: 3yr/$24M with $7M signing bonus (primary guarantee). Thomas contract per Spotrac/OTC: 2yr/$8M with $3M guaranteed.
